results.title: NIST Engineering Statistics Handbook
results.description: The system was verified and tested for all modules and proved to be accurate for small objects measurement. The traceability and uncertainty of the introduced system has been expressed and the expanded uncertainty was calculated as 9.99 µm ...
results.content:
The system was verified and tested for all modules and proved to be accurate for small objects measurement. The traceability and uncertainty of the introduced system has been expressed and the expanded uncertainty was calculated as 9.99 µm ...
Manufacturers must produce leads which are thin and flexible, for patient comfort, while being highly reliable as repair requires surgical intervention. Reliable leads contribute to a better quality of life, and reduce the trauma and expense of the surgeries required to replace failures, across the patient population.
In order to estimate maintenance cost for the manufacturing industry as a whole , a sufficient sample size is needed. Depending on the standard deviation, confidence interval, and accepted margin of error, a needed sample size of 77 is estimated, but could reasonably be as low as 14.
The system was verified and tested for all modules and proved to be accurate for small objects measurement. The traceability and uncertainty of the introduced system has been expressed and the expanded uncertainty was calculated as 9.99 µm (maximum error = ±5 µm), which provide a 95% level of confidence.
[22] and the expanded uncertainty (U c ) was calculated as ku c , where k is the coverage factor. The degree of freedom (v) of the combined uncertainty was calculated using Equation 5 [23] and it was found equal to 12. Therefore, the coverage factor k was taken equal to 2.23 to achieve a level of confidence = 95%. ...

results.title: Using the Cumulative Binomial Equation for Reliability Demonstration Test Design and for Estimating the Parameters of a Data Set with Zero Failures
results.description: Because there are zero failures in the test, Eqn. (1) becomes: Let us use the Weibull distribution for this example. Given that all five units survived 30 hours, we use the following equation: Using an assumed β value of 1.8 and a confidence level of 0.9 in the above equation:
results.content:
Because there are zero failures in the test, Eqn. (1) becomes: Let us use the Weibull distribution for this example. Given that all five units survived 30 hours, we use the following equation: Using an assumed β value of 1.8 and a confidence level of 0.9 in the above equation:
In this article, we will introduce the cumulative binomial equation and explore two potential applications for reliability engineering. First, we will explain how the equation can be applied for designing a Reliability Demonstration Test (RDT) that will be effective for demonstrating that a certain product has met or exceeded a given reliability at a given confidence interval. For this application, the equation can be adapted to apply to a situation in which no failures are expected to occur in the test.
Distributions commonly used to describe the failure time include the Weibull, lognormal and exponential distributions. For example, suppose that the goal is to demonstrate that the reliability at time t meets the required reliability RL(t) at a specified confidence level, CL.
Figure 1: Enter the value of beta and the confidence level. Figure 2: Calculated value of eta. Case 2: Assume that you have field data from four products and the observation period ended at a different time for each one. Again, no failures were observed.
